coredata.dc:description: Understanding and modeling lithium-ion battery degradation is essential for optimized use but remains a significant challenge, with various aging mechanisms proposed in recent years. Towards a holistic, physics-based model description, the complex interrelationships among aging mechanisms, parameter identification, and experimental validation are highly active research areas. This work presents findings from a one-year, 1000-cycle aging study using multi-reference electrode single-layer pouch cells with NMC-811/graphite. These were utilized to validate a physics-based degradation model based on the Doyle-Fuller-Newman framework, leveraging half-cell-resolved measurement data in a pre-plating regime. The validated model is applied to investigate the influence of different aging mechanisms on the anode’s surface potential, a key parameter for avoiding lithium plating during charging. Additionally, the effects of degradation on anode-potential limited fast charging capability are discussed. Loss of active material is identified as a key mechanism which, if not considered, results in overestimation of the anode potential. Additionally, a significant resistance increase in the cathode material, which alters cell performance during aging, was identified and empirically captured in the model. Without reference electrodes, this resistance increase could not be accurately detected and, as suggested in the literature, would likely have been misattributed to the anode in the aging model.

Q
LAM
for the first RPT is annotated. Broken lines refer to the identified features in the DVA, used for peak tracking.

Gray background denotes full-cell utilization window. 500 cycles until end of simulation were taken into account for the LLI calculation. Only the offsets of the first RPT are shown for readability. Dotted lines refer to measured curves, dashed to scaled OCP curves. DVA of measured negative half-cell curve, obtained from a 0.2 C charging regime in RPT (a). DVA of scaled, pristine OCP curve of the negative half-cell (b). DVA of measured positive half-cell curve, obtained from a 0.2 C charging regime in RPT (c). DVA of scaled, pristine OCP curve of the positive half-cell (d). Progression of the available capacity, where the upper bar denotes the positive electrode, the lower bar describes the negative electrode (e).

Each row shows either half-cell or full-cell results. Dotted lines represent the absolute modeling error. Simulative behavior of the proposed aging model after 500 cycles of simulated aging with 0.5 C charging and discharging. Charging at 0.2 C (a)–(b), 0.5 C (c)–(d), and 0.75 C (e)–(f), neglecting particle crack propagation (blue), LAM (green), and SEI growth (orange) submodels. The magnification subplots demonstrate the consequences of excluding the respective submodels when predicting the anode potential. (b), (d) and (f) depict the absolute deviation from the reference simulation (red, dashed) in dotted lines.

MCC charging initiated at 1.5 C with various simulation configurations for impact analysis, neglecting the indicated aging submodels of the previously proposed aging-sensitive model. Simulation is conducted after 500 cycles of aging at predominantly 0.5 C charge/discharge. The current is recursively reduced by 1% upon reaching the 20 mV threshold, resulting in a steadily decreasing C-rate during MCC (a). Model-predicted negative electrode potential at the separator interface (b), model-predicted positive electrode potential at the separator interface (c), and model-predicted full-cell voltage (d).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding and modeling lithium-ion battery degradation is essential for optimized use but remains a significant challenge, with various aging mechanisms proposed in recent years. Towards a holistic, physics-based model description, the complex interrelationships among aging mechanisms, parameter identification, and experimental validation are highly active research areas. This work presents findings from a one-year, 1000-cycle aging study using multi-reference electrode single-layer pouch cells with NMC-811/graphite. These were utilized to validate a physics-based degradation model based on the Doyle-Fuller-Newman framework, leveraging half-cell-resolved measurement data in a pre-plating regime. The validated model is applied to investigate the influence of different aging mechanisms on the anode’s surface potential, a key parameter for avoiding lithium plating during charging. Additionally, the effects of degradation on anode-potential limited fast charging capability are discussed. Loss of active material is identified as a key mechanism which, if not considered, results in overestimation of the anode potential. Additionally, a significant resistance increase in the cathode material, which alters cell performance during aging, was identified and empirically captured in the model. Without reference electrodes, this resistance increase could not be accurately detected and, as suggested in the literature, would likely have been misattributed to the anode in the aging model.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As lithium-ion battery technology continues to evolve, both academic research and industry are striving to increase the energy density and longevity of battery cells. Despite great practically demonstrated success in terms of peak performance, calendar and cycle life, a comprehensive understanding of the underlying aging mechanisms in batteries is still a subject of ongoing research.

In highly demanding applications such as Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs), with designs that aim to maximize energy density, fast charging capability, safety, as well as to minimize costs, the limited understanding of the actual aging behavior of cells remains a notable challenge. It impedes the development of precise state estimation and health-aware control algorithms for the Battery Management System (BMS), which is reflected in the significant tolerance bands for, e.g., the estimation of State of Health (SoH) or remaining useful life in regulatory frameworks such as the EURO 7 norm [1,2]. To address this challenge, researchers have developed a variety of models aimed at simulating and understanding experimentally observed aging mechanisms, which can be categorized into the broader classes of interface passivation, e.g., lithium plating or Solid-Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) growth, and mechanical fatigue, e.g., leading to Loss of Active Material (LAM) or particle crack propagation [3–5].

Nevertheless, these models frequently depend on parameters that are challenging to observe and identify, particularly using conventional full-cell setups. A popular approach for obtaining a deeper understanding of the aging behavior of different lithium-ion cell types involves harvesting electrode material from aged cells and further testing them in half-cell configuration [6–9]. However, this approach is associated with procedural difficulties, such as the extraction of electrolyte from commercial cells, often making the usage of different electrolytes inevitable, and difficulties in obtaining viable coin cells from significantly degraded commercial cells due to mechanical fatigue of the material. A more advanced in-situ measurement technique involves inserting Reference Electrodes (REs) directly into the cells under investigation [10–12], enabling the recording of half-cell data without the need for active material harvesting. Notwithstanding its advantages, the utilization of in-situ half-cell data collected over extended cycling for the detailed validation of aging models remains rare in literature, despite the highly active discussion of aging modeling: semi-empirical half-cell models are often used to derive degradation modes via fitting mostly with half-cell curves of pristine material, thus providing the theoretical basis for electrochemical modeling [8,13–15]. In electrochemical modeling, accounting for first-principle influence of interface passivation is often the primarily considered aging effect [16–19]. However, several authors emphasize the need for mechanical coupling and integrating fatigue of passivation layers or particles into, e.g., SEI models [20–24]. The underlying modeled mechanical fatigue process is also used to account for capacity fade from LAM and stress-induced diffusion changes within the electrodes [25,26].

Another relevant point, being addressed by the insertion of REs, is the possibility of validating battery models serving as virtual sensors for half-cell potential controlled fast charging [27]. During charging, the negative electrode is generally considered a susceptible component of the battery cell, especially under conditions such as high current densities or low temperatures [28,29]. Literature reports that lithium plating becomes thermodynamically feasible when the negative electrode potential drops below 0 V vs.
Li/Li
+
, causing reduced lithium intercalation into the graphite in favor of metallic deposition at the electrode’s surface [30–34]. This phenomenon must be avoided under all operating conditions, and even more so as the cell ages.

The aging effects, however, further complicate the use of real-time capable electrochemical models as a virtual sensor, as full integration of various aging submodels seems impractical. Further, it remains unclear to what extent quantities of interest, such as the negative half-cell potential, are affected by additional kinetic and transport limitations. This uncertainty may increase the risk of lithium plating due to overestimation of the negative half-cell potential, caused by inaccurate modeling, which in turn can lead to accelerated degradation of battery performance and longevity issues.

To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this aspect of comprehensive modeling of the negative electrode behavior with respect to aging effects and their implications for anode potential-controlled charging strategies has not yet been discussed in the literature. While previous DFN-based degradation studies have established comprehensive mechanistic frameworks, existing aging models have not been thoroughly investigated and validated using half-cell-resolved data from extensive cycling, which fundamentally improves confidence in model predictions, as it substantially reduces the ambiguity inherent in fitting models to full-cell data alone, where multiple parameter combinations and effects can produce similar terminal responses, while yielding vastly different internal states.

Therefore, this work aims (i) to describe and analyze the experimental aging study conducted on SLP cells with multiple REs, focusing on the REs’ stability and the half-cells’ inherent degradation mechanisms in pre-plating conditions, (ii) to introduce the methods and a state-of-the-art aging model, followed by its parameterization using the experimental half-cell-resolved data to accurately incorporate the individual aging behaviors of the half-cells, and (iii) to systematically investigate which degradation mechanisms most significantly impact the anode potential through a impact analysis, based on the aging-sensitive DFN model derived and calibrated by the half-cell data, which is important for the practical application of such models for avoidance of lithium plating and ensure safe operational use.

By addressing these points, this work contributes to bridging the gap between the theoretical modeling and experimental validation of lithium-ion cell degradation, and its relevance to application, which has not been examined in previous work. It provides important insights into the most impactful and essential degradation mechanisms for developing aging-sensitive models on resource-constrained platforms, valuable for virtual sensing and other advanced BMS tasks, where highly complex models with consideration of a multitude of aging mechanisms cannot be fully integrated. Therefore, rather than aiming for a broadly generalizable and statistically robust model, the focus of this study is on identifying the most relevant aging mechanisms to achieve practical accuracy.

In the present study, custom SLP cells with a nominal capacity of 100 mAh and 25
cm
2
electrode area, featuring graphite on the negative electrode and NMC-811 on the positive electrode were utilized. The electrolyte comprised 1.0 M Lithium Hexafluorophosphate (
LiPF
6
) and an additional 0.2 M Lithium Bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide (LiFSI) dissolved in a mixture of Ethylene Carbonate (EC), Dimethylcarbonate (DMC), and Ethylmethylcarbonate (EMC) with a weight ratio of 1:1:1.

For a detailed description of the SLP cell, readers are referred, for the sake of brevity, to the dedicated publication on the manufacturing process of the SLP cells by Oehler et al. [35], including detailed information on the layout of the cell, the positioning of the REs, the formation protocol, and used materials. For the reader’s convenience, a brief summary is provided below.

Prior to assembly, the negative electrode, with a loading of 4.4
mAh
cm
− 2
, was calendered to a porosity of 36.9% to achieve a thickness of approximately 83
μ m
. The cathode active material, with a loading of 4.0
mAh
cm
− 2
and 32.5% porosity, yielded a thickness of approximately 70
μ m
. After cutting, removing the coating from one side of the sheet, and tab welding, the negative electrode was placed on aluminum-laminated pouch foil, followed by the REs embedded between two glass fiber separator layers, and finally the positive electrode. In addition to the
Li
4
Ti
5
O
12
Reference Electrode (LTO-RE) located outside the stack, four Gold Wire Reference Electrodes (GWRE) were evenly distributed between two glass fiber layers inside the stack.

After assembly and vacuum drying, the dry cells were transferred to an argon-filled glove box, filled with 1750
μ l
of electrolyte and soaked repeatedly to ensure optimal wetting of the electrodes and separator layers before heat sealing.

For long-term cycling, a temperature test chamber (Vötsch VTS 7018-5, Weiss Technik GmbH, Germany) maintained thermal conditioning at 25 °C during cycling and Reference Performance Tests (RPTs). Stable temperature and pressure distributions throughout the entire test period were ensured by custom steel fixtures compatible with the REs, as illustrated and detailed in the work of Oehler et al. [35].

The repetitive charging and discharging for cyclic load was performed using source meters (Keithley 2460, Tektronix, Inc., USA) and a 6.5-digit digital multimeter (Keithley 2000) with a multiplexer card to measure the LTO-RE potential at a sampling frequency of
≈
0 . 7
Hz
. The instruments were controlled by custom automation software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic aging and reference performance tests
ce:section.ce:para: The cells underwent repeated charging at a Constant Current (CC) rate of 0.5 C, transitioned to Constant Voltage (CV) mode once 4.2 V was reached and terminated when the current dropped below C/50, followed by 0.5 C CC discharging until 2.8 V. This charge/discharge cycle was repeated 50 times between each RPT, with the cycling window set from 2.8 V to 4.2 V. The charging and discharging currents during cycling were not altered throughout the study; the C-rate was set based on the full-cell discharge capacity after formation and three full cycles for capacity stabilization.

In accordance with findings by Geslin et al. [36], who demonstrate that CC discharge typically represents a worst-case scenario for cell capacity retention and often underestimates the cell’s lifetime compared to more realistic, dynamic discharge, the present study employed CC discharge to specifically investigate a worst-case scenario.

The RPTs consisted of two full charge/discharge cycles at 0.2 C, followed by one full cycle at 0.75 C. The State of Charge (SoC) was then set to 50% (capacity controlled), and 10 s DC pulse resistance measurements were performed at 1.0 C in both charge and discharge directions. Considering the cells’ capacity stabilization and initial break-in processes, the first RPT after 50 cycles is designated as the initial RPT, and used for subsequent analyses as the baseline. The LTO-RE, located outside the active electrode area, was used to measure the half-cell potentials due to its larger capacity and intrinsic electrochemical stability [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of the LTO reference electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Since the half-cell data collected from the presented testing procedure fundamentally rely on the electrochemical stability of the LTO-RE, it is essential to verify the long-term stability of the LTO-RE’s potential in the given system. Oehler et al. [35] previously demonstrated that both the GWREs and the LTO-RE exhibit stable reference potentials for more than 10 months when using the described cell manufacturing process and cell layout.

Besides this work, other studies have not provided detailed observations on the long-term stability of comparable setups. For instance, Costard et al. [37] found that LTO-coated on an aluminum mesh maintains a stable reference potential for at least 70 days. Additionally, Epding et al. [38] reported that recharging an LTO-coated aluminum mesh used as a RE was necessary to maintain a stable reference potential throughout aging experiments with SLP cells. However, the previously investigated time spans are insufficient for the presented aging study, which lasted for about a year. Furthermore, instability due to variance in cell production of the herein investigated cells must be ruled out.

Therefore, the half-cell potential curves measured against the inserted LTO-RE during charging at 0.2 C are analyzed under the assumption that the overpotentials, which are reported to be in the magnitude of 10−2 V for graphite-based anodes [39], are negligibly low at that current level, and any anomalous potential drift in the LTO-RE over the course of the experiment would be detectable as an abnormal potential drop or drift. Fig. 1a shows the measured potentials curves, and, for comparison, the pristine Open Circuit Potential (OCP) curve of the negative electrode in dashed light blue, measured with Galvanostatic Intermittent Titration Technique (GITT). Despite the expected decrease in CC charging capacity, the negative electrode potential remains apparently stable, as shown in Fig. 1a.

To assess the electrochemical stability of the LTO-RE in a more detailed, numerical manner, a reference SoC at ≈ 8% ( ≈ 6.5 mAh) was chosen for comparison. The Differential Voltage Analysis (DVA) in Fig. 1b shows a stable feature at this SoC over 1000 cycles, indicating that degradation effects on the cell balancing and thus the negative electrode potential are minimal at this point. As previously noted by Oehler et al. [35] and Bach et al. [40] for GWREs, potentiostatic instability, such as lithiation or delithiation of the LTO-RE due to unsuitable measuring equipment or manufacturing issues, would result in an upward or downward drift in the measured half-cell potentials over time. Fig. 1c confirms that this is not the case by comparing the difference of the baseline 0.2 C charging curve of the negative half-cell after 50 cycles with the 0.2 C charging curves during the entire test period at the defined reference point of ≈ 6.5 mAh. Given the negative electrode’s degradation, the maximum difference of less than 4 mV can be deemed negligible and the RE is considered stable.

However, the low deviation could be due to mutually canceling mechanisms. Increasing overpotentials from side reactions could lower the negative electrode’s potential during charging. Conversely, if delithiation occurred due to measurement equipment, an increase in the LTO-RE’s potential is expected. This scenario is deemed unlikely with the utilized setup, given the multimeter’s high input impedance of > 100
G Ω
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limitations of the experimental aging study
ce:section.ce:para: In line with good scientific practice, it is important to note that the aging study with initially four SLPs faced external challenges, resulting in two early failures and instability in one cell’s LTO-RE. Consequently, the model validation primarily relied on data from one cell that reached the end of test criterion after 1000 cycles. While the limited sample size restricts the statistical power and unconditional generalizability of our findings in terms of establishing universal parameter values, it is important to emphasize that this is not the primary objective of this work. Rather, we aim to demonstrate how resource efficiency, accuracy, and complexity in aging modeling can be systematically assessed, balanced, and weighted, which is particularly relevant for applications on resource-constrained platforms, such as, e.g., virtual sensing.

The restricted nature of the dataset is, to some extent, offset by the considerable depth of observability enabled by the RE, which provides direct access to cell-internal metrics of interest, which is in our case predominately the anode potential. These metrics, which are of uttermost importance for modeling would be otherwise inaccessible. Conceptualizing, building, and operating controlled, long-term aging of such cells with REs is exceptionally demanding and inherently prone to complications, as evidenced by the premature failure of the other cells, yet they provide valuable insights into electrode-level aging that are rarely available in long-term aging studies.

Nevertheless, we acknowledge that validation across multiple cells under diverse operating conditions would be necessary to confirm broader applicability of the specific parameter values obtained. Future work should therefore focus on expanding the dataset to establish the model’s generalizability across different cells and operating conditions.

The availability of half-cell measurements allowed for a re-evaluation of the common practice of fitting pristine half-cell OCP curves to degraded full-cell Open Circuit Voltage (OCV) curves for Degradation Mode Analysis (DMA).

For the DMA and pooling of individual physical aging phenomena into Loss of Lithium Inventory ( LLI ),
LAM
neg
, and
LAM
pos
, we opted not to use the well-established OCV-fitting concept presented by, e.g., Schmitt et al. [7,8], in favor of a broader approach enabled by the LTO-RE. This approach eliminates numerical fitting procedures by directly utilizing in-situ half-cell data for straightforward interpretation of electrode-specific degradation.

In terms of DMA, Christensen and Newman [13] laid a crucial foundation by investigating unequal capacity loss in the electrodes, distinguishing between LLI and capacity fade by attributing changes in cycle path slopes to shifting lithiation windows. Their concept of scaling and shifting cell balancing curves was refined and adopted by numerous authors. For instance, Birkl et al. [15] assumed that the inherent features in both electrodes’ OCP curves remain constant over lifetime and proposed an algorithm to determine LLI,
LAM
neg
, and
LAM
pos
using slow charge/discharge curves, optimizing lithiation and delithiation windows through adjustments to half-cell curves.

In contrast, other authors do not use numerical fitting, but analyze the evolution of characteristic peaks arising in curves from analytical methods such as Incremental Capacity Analysis (ICA) or DVA to track degradation patterns [41–44]. Following this approach, the work of Dubarry et al. [45] shows that the shift of phase transitions and resulting DVA peaks, indicative of degradation, can be quantified relative to degradation-insensitive features to track LAM and LLI over the course of aging. Consistent with this understanding of degradation modes and the availability of half-cell potential curves, the idea of analytical peak tracking in readily available DVA curves is pursued in the present study, but in close relation to the often chosen OCV fitting approach.

As outlined in Section 2.3, the employed RPT incorporated a slow charging routine at 0.2 C, which is notably higher than the C-rate chosen by other researchers [42,44,46]. Nevertheless, our choice, made with regard to the long test duration, is consistent with the practical implications for BMS, where charging rates below 0.1 C are impractical, particularly in automotive applications, and the typical charging power rate for EVs is 11 kW, completing the charging process in 4 to 8 h depending on the system capacity [47]. However, polarization undoubtedly increases at higher C-rates, which can lead to ambiguous features in the DVA and, in turn, may necessitate the implementation of resistance correction. Still, Fly et al. [48], for instance, found sufficient peak distinguishability with ICA for DMA at about 0.167 C on NCA and LFP cells, which aligns with the chosen C-rate of 0.2 C for the present study’s small-scale, and thus presumably homogeneous, SLP cell [35].

Fig. 2 illustrates the calculation of LAM, following the previously outlined peak tracking approach for charging. Since there is no final consensus in the literature regarding the nomenclature of graphite phases, the following derivations are based on the definition proposed by Gantenbein et al. [49–51]. As aging of the cell progresses, stage II – representing the phase transitions of I/II and II/III of the negative electrode’s graphite – shifts to the left relative to the reference feature of stage III, which indicates the transition from phase II/III to phase III/IV. In combination with the previously described assumption of constant active material properties, the decrease of
Q

Besides the determination of LAM, the estimation of LLI is necessary for the validation of the proposed aging model, which is discussed in Section 3.2. Following the peak tracking approach that has been utilized previously, for the sake of illustration, the LLI determination is delineated with reference to the negative half-cell, employing the phase transition from II to III. Again, we refer to the notation of Gantenbein et al. [49–51].

To ensure readability, only the vertical lines for the first RPT are shown. Bow tie markers represent a locked reference point for LAM scaling [55]. By analogous calculation for the positive half-cell, shown in Fig. 3c and Fig. 3d, the lithiation window can be traced for both half-cells, taking into account LAM and the lateral feature shift

To validate and compare the outlined methodology, a scaling and shifting of the initially measured half-cell voltage curves to the respective degraded voltage curves in accordance to the determined degradation modes is necessary. Due to the elevated C-rate during the RPTs, aging-induced resistance growth must be taken into account when fitting the baseline reference charge curve to the degraded charge curves with the obtained LLI and LAM values. For this purpose, Fly et al. [48] propose a simple approximation by linear refinement of the measured charging curves through interpolation of the obtained IR drop values at the beginning of the CC charging and the end of CV charging.

It should be noted, that Rodrigues et al. [56] report that degradation can alter the structure of nickel-rich layered oxides, consequently altering the properties of the positive electrode over the course of aging. To address this issue, they propose blending of the aged half-cell curve with the pristine one for the studied active material consisting of
LiNi

Mn

2
. However, this was not necessary in the presented case to obtain reasonable results with NMC-811. As shown in Fig. A.1, scaling and shifting the baseline potential curves with LAM and LLI values from the proposed peak tracking method with the IR drop refinement successfully reproduces the deteriorated potential curves at the end of simulation after 500 cycles. The curves show good agreement, confirming the chosen approach.

The proposed simulation model must consider various mechanisms of degradation to properly account for the predominantly cyclic aging in the experimental study. Prior work has identified numerous processes contributing to cell degradation and particularly negative electrode aging, such as interface passivation and mechanical fatigue, highlighting the complexity of lithium-ion battery aging and the need for extended research. This is particularly evident for advanced validation, since REs are rarely used in long-term aging experiments, thus limiting in-situ analyses to the full-cell voltage and capacity retention. To contribute, the aging model presented in this study focuses on passivation layer growth, particle cracking, LAM, and their relationship to the negative electrode potential, with validation based on half-cell data. The presented model does not consider lithium plating, since the experimental study was limited to C-rates that are expected to refrain the negative half-cell potential from reaching critical levels.

Throughout aging, batteries undergo an increase in internal resistance [60–62]. This effect is partly assumed to be caused by the growth of a passivation layer formed at the interface between active material and electrolyte, which consumes cyclable lithium and solvents in the electrolyte. The phenomenon has been studied intensively, especially for the negative electrode [13,63,64]. However, the positive electrode is likely also affected by passivation processes, which gradually change its kinetics [65]. To be consistent with the literature’s nomenclature, the passivation layers are referred to as positive electrode Solid Electrolyte Interphase (pSEI) and negative electrode Solid Electrolyte Interphase (nSEI).

Various approaches exist for modeling this side reaction, focussing primarily on the nSEI under the assumption that the solvent diffusion through the nSEI is rate-limiting over time, while neglecting potential deviations throughout calendar or cyclic aging [66]. However, this approach does not align with the observed capacity fade in experimental studies and must therefore be extended by SoC-dependent effects, such that the potential influences the rate of nSEI growth [67]. To account for this, the proposed model utilizes a nSEI growth model, in which lithium-ion diffusion within the nSEI is rate-limiting, yet potential-dependent. [18,66,67].

While focusing on the negative electrode, the model must account for positive electrode resistance increases to accurately capture its voltage curve and full-cell capacity.

However, a complete mechanistic replication of cathode degradation is challenging due to its inherent complexity. For example, layered lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxides are known to undergo particle phase changes upon oxygen release, which are assumed to play a major role in impedance growth [68,69]. To model these effects in a physically meaningful way, Ghosh et al. [70] proposed a DFN-type model with a dedicated, numerically resolved passivation phase in the particles, which was further investigated by Zhuo et al. [71]. Still, these models only replicate the phase changes of the active material in isolation and ignore other effects, such as mechanical coupling. They are also numerically expensive and require hitherto unclear model calibration. In view of these difficulties and the research question outlined in Section 1, these models were not adopted in this study. Instead, the increase in polarization at the positive electrode is modeled under the assumption of pSEI formation using a physically supported but empirically interpreted approach based on the solvent-diffusion limited SEI model [67,72], that is easier to parameterize and numerically efficient.

Beyond capacity fade caused by the growth of initially existent passivation layers, research indicates that mechanical fatigue of the active material plays a crucial role in battery aging. Accordingly, the proposed model accounts for various, mechanically coupled degradation mechanisms with a particular focus on the negative electrode. During lithiation and delithiation, the active material undergoes volume changes that lead to tensile and compressive stress and eventually to mechanical failure in the form of cracking [50,62,73,74]. Based on the work of Zang et al. [75], Ai et al. [26], Laresgoiti et al. [21], and Deshpande et al. [20] the model presented includes these processes in form of a mechanical model, introduced in Eqs.((9) – (11)).

Assuming isotropic, spherical particles, as is commonly done in DFN-type models, these equations reveal a coupling between lithium concentration, volume change, and mechanical stress within the particles. Greater deviations in local lithium concentration from the particle’s average concentration result in increased mechanical stress. This stress triggers several aging mechanisms, which must be accounted for in the proposed model.

Stress-induced diffusion: If active material particles are exposed to mechanical stress, the diffusivity at the surface of the particles of the active material changes [5,26]. This behavior interferes with the previously introduced mechanical stress model since elevated diffusion will likely cause smaller concentration gradients. The proposed simulation model accounts for stress-induced diffusion with an additional diffusivity coefficient

Modeling the formation of SEI on cracks, herein referenced as secondary SEI, is a subject of ongoing discussion. According to Karger et al. [24], SEI formation due to aging can be divided into three theories: (i) SEI cracking [22,78], (ii) SEI defoliation [74,79], and (iii) SEI formation due to particle cracking [72]. For the proposed model, it is assumed that nSEI formation on freshly exposed graphite due to crack propagation is the dominant mechanism, in line with Karger et al. and that SEI cracking and SEI defoliation are either negligible or of little consequence. Under this assumption, we adopt the modeling approach of Deshpande et al. [20] and assume a constant crack distribution with a constant crack width

Another effect associated with the formation of SEI is a decrease in the porosity of the active material [80]. Since porosity is a crucial factor for ionic transport, it can be concluded that a decrease could lead to higher overpotentials and thus lower potential of the negative electrode during lithiation [81,82]. ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical nature of the aging models used and inherent modeling assumptions
ce:section.ce:para: Although the presented aging model is based on physical principles, it simplifies the complex aging processes of lithium-ion batteries. Common simplifications in battery modeling stem from dimensional reductions, parameter uncertainties, and mechanistic assumptions. Aging models must balance complexity, accuracy, computational efficiency, and application relevance, depending on the research question and intended use.

The proposed model reconciles these factors by combining mechanistic submodels, such as particle mechanics, with empirically interpreted approaches, such as the pSEI model. This combination enables reasonable predictions of cell aging behavior with physical interpretability while maintaining computational efficiency for practical applications. It is imperative to acknowledge that the aging model’s accuracy can be directly influenced by the validity of the underlying assumptions, which may span from the choice of the DFN model, that inherently simplifies the cell’s geometry, for example, to the dimensionally reduced representation of particle mechanics, and the assumption of isothermal conditions. Considering this study’s application-focused objective, the comparatively high homogeneity of the SLP cells previously demonstrated [35], and the aforementioned modeling trade-offs, the assumptions made are deemed reasonable, effectively balancing the competing demands of model complexity and accuracy.

The parameters of degradation models are often challenging or impossible to determine through direct measurement techniques. Consequently, parameters are typically estimated by iterative fitting of observable performance metrics. However, this approach risks suboptimal parameter identification. Laue et al. [84] highlight overfitting risks and parametric sensitivity variance as critical issues, even when solely parameterizing the standard DFN model. Recognizing these limitations, the proposed model adopts the aging parameter set from O’Kane et al. [72] as baseline. Adopting literature parameters is common for complex, highly coupled models to reduce parameters under investigation, while maintaining model robustness. Furthermore, the LTO-RE provides enhanced insights into cell behavior, enabling distinct separation of aging effects and polarization changes for both electrodes. This represents progress towards more identifiable degradation parameter fitting without risks associated with electrode harvesting. The validation data is summarized in Table 1.

In the SEI submodels,
D
nSEI
and
D
pSEI
were fitted, given their ambiguity in literature and their direct impact on LLI, capacity loss, and overpotential increase. Furthermore, the ionic SEI resistivity
R
SEI
was reduced by 30%, relative to
2

5

m
, proposed by Borodin et al. [85] and Safari et al. [16], who used an electrolyte consisting of EC-DEC-DMC (1:1:1) and 1 M LiPF6. However, this value might be smaller with the addition of LiFSI in EC:DMC:EMC in the presented SLP’s electrolyte. By comparing
LiPF
6
and LiFSI in a carbonate solvent mixture of EC, Propylene Carbonate (PC), and DMC, Asheim et al. [86] experimentally found that the SEI formed from the latter exhibits better ionic conductivity, indicating that
R
SEI
in the investigated SLP might be smaller than
2
⋅

.

Moreover, the particle crack propagation model parameters were adjusted due to a lack of consensus in the literature regarding operating conditions and circumstances that lead to particle cracking. For instance, Takahashi et al. [87] report that at 25 °C cracking of graphite particles is rather unlikely. However, the authors do not consider initial defects within the graphite particles, which are expected to grow at less severe cycling conditions and, therefore, lower mechanical stress [25,26,88]. Hence, in the outlined crack propagation submodel,
k

and
ρ
cr
are the relevant parameters. For

, different values have been proposed in the literature. However, in this work, we use the value of

Additionally, the empirical LAM model described in Eq. (18) was fitted with the parameter

was obtained from O’Kane et al. [72].

Despite integrating various types of data at different observation levels into the model’s parameterization to enhance the experimental observability of aging effects and constrain the model to data on multiple levels, such as half-cell-specific kinetic signatures and electrode-balancing changes, a parametric sensitivity analysis may be helpful in identifying compensating parameters and further clarifying degradation pathways within electrodes. Unfortunately, as of today, no such parametric analyses exist for the presented model or comparable ones in the literature, which might be primarily due to the methodological and computational complexity of determining the contributions of individual parameters with respect to outcome quantities. This complexity can be attributed to the DFN model’s nonlinear nature and inherent parameter interactions, which make sensitivity assessment with isolated parameter variation around a nominal value difficult [89–91], even without considering aging effects. While the current study cannot address this challenge due to scope limitations and methodological ambiguities, the authors are currently developing strategies for global parametric sensitivity analysis of an aging-sensitive DFN model. This analysis will help clarify the model’s parametric sensitivity and, in progression, provide further insights into its parametric identifiability, as sensitivity results inform but do not fully resolve identifiability, which is a limitation explicitly acknowledged in the present work.

Using the described aging submodels to extend the DFN model, initially parameterized and introduced by Oehler et al. [27] under usage of parameters of Table A.1, the agreement of the model with measurements of the investigated SLP under pristine condition must be evaluated, since, e.g., particle mechanics might alter the model’s kinetics. To maintain consistency with prior publications, we compare the proposed model to results from Oehler et al. [35], who investigated inhomogeneities across the SLPs of the same type (NMC-811/graphite) with GWREs and a LTO-RE.

Fig. 4 displays the extended model’s agreement with measurements of SLP ① from [35], which is functionally equivalent to the SLP cell presented here. In addition to GWRE 1 and 4, the potential of the LTO-RE is also shown, which differs significantly from the potential of the GWREs due to the spatial allocation, as outlined in Section 2.1. From Fig. 4, it can be derived that the simulation model, originally introduced to replicate the GWREs’ potential, does not match the LTO-RE’s potential, which was predominantly recorded due to considerations of electrochemical long-term stability.

To replicate the LTO-RE’s potential, we introduce an ohmic factor
R
GWRE → LTO−RE
to the model with Eq. (21), aiming to map the electrolyte resistance between the position of the GWREs and the LTO-RE, where I is the applied current.
R
GWRE → LTO−RE
is
0 . 35
Ω
.
(21)
U =
ϕ

−
ϕ
l
+ I ⋅
R
GWRE→LTO−RE ,

The proposed model, which assumes constant electrolyte characteristics throughout aging is illustrated in Fig. 4 in green. It accurately matches the LTO-RE potential curve and is therefore used in the following.

After analyzing the LTO-RE stability, discussing the experimental setup, and presenting the modeling approach, the aging-sensitive model is compared to measurements. The impact of degradation submodels on anode potential is then assessed using One-At-A-Time (OAAT) analyses for constant C-rates and an optimized fast charging regime.

By iterative fitting over a multitude of simulations, the parameters listed in Table A.1 were obtained, resulting in the model accuracy shown in Fig. 5. The columns compare different C-rates, while the rows show full-cell and half-cell agreement. The end of simulation was set to 500 cycles due to computational time constraints. Annotations highlight the overpotential progression, with the model reproducing half-cell curves well using the chosen parameters. The simulated positive half-cell potentials show significant overpotential increases, yet deviate more from the measurements than the negative half-cell potentials. This is likely due to the complex degradation behavior of the NMC-811 cathode material, which is not captured in its full physicochemical complexity by the chosen aging submodels, as discussed in Section 3.2.5 and Section 3.2.1. However, it is important to underscore that the resistance increase at the positive electrode only becomes identifiable, specifiable in the accompanying model, and ultimately parametrizable through the experimental setup with a RE presented in this work. To the best of our knowledge, this level of electrode-resolved model calibration has not been previously demonstrated in the context of aging models.

Besides the comparison of simulated against measured half-cell and full-cell curves, degradation modes and capacity retention must match the experimental data. As discussed by Li et al. [92], degradation modes are important metrics for reducing overfitting risks in physics-based aging models. Fig. 6 therefore illustrates the agreement between simulated and experimentally extracted degradation modes from Section 3.1, plotted over Equivalent Full Cycles (EFCs). The charge capacities
Q
CC
and
Q
CCCV
are also shown, deviating only approximately 1.5 mAh .

Overall, the model agreement is satisfactory, particularly considering that this study leverages in-situ half-cell potentials, which is rarely available in literature. However, it remains possible that the chosen parameters and aging submodels may not fully account for the underlying, physically entangled mechanisms, which are difficult to uniquely discern. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, though, no aging-sensitive model in literature has yet been validated in-situ in such depth with REs and without active material harvesting over a long-term period.

With the satisfactory agreement between the simulation and measurements, Fig. 7 assesses which modeled mechanisms are most impactful for accurately modeling the negative half-cell potential during charging. This is done by successively deactivating the individual aging submodels included in the previously proposed model. The charging C-rates were selected based on those used in the experimental aging study and the model fitting process, which approximate a realistic usage scenario without risking lithium plating. In contrast to the previous analysis of the RE potentials using the LTO-RE model, the negative electrode potential is now evaluated specifically at the anode-separator interface, since this region is expected to exhibit the lowest local potentials and thus has the highest risk of lithium plating. [27].

Fig. 7 illustrates the previously introduced, simulated charging scheme of 0.2 C, 0.5 C, and 0.75 C after 500 cycles of symmetric charging and discharging with 0.5 C. The simulation, which does not account for particle crack propagation, is nearly indistinguishable from the reference simulation that incorporates all of the previously described aging submodels. The comparison between the dashed red half-cell curve of the reference model and the blue solid curve of the OAAT model without particle crack propagation in Fig. 7 implies that particle crack propagation is unlikely to be the determining mechanism of degradation under the investigated aging conditions and the chosen parameters in the model. However, with the cell under study and its inherent high-energy design, long-term cycling at higher C-rates to further investigate this behavior for the negative electrode is not feasible. This may be the reason why the crack propagation submodel does not significantly impact degradation in the model, given its parameterization for moderate C-rates, as discussed in Section 3.3.1.

In the scenario of neglecting SEI growth, and under the assumption of a constant initial passivation layer thickness for both the negative and positive electrode, the potential curve of the negative electrode, depicted in orange in Fig. 7, exhibits greater disparities as the charging C-rate increases. Consequently, the full-cell cut-off voltage of 4.2 V is reached later, due to the smaller polarization on the positive electrode. This, in turn, causes the potential of the negative electrode to decrease further and approach the 20 mV threshold, due to longer lithiation, as annotated in Fig. 7e for 0.75 C. However, despite the prolonged lithiation of the negative electrode, the half-cell potential curve of the model in orange does not demonstrate significant vertical shifts compared to the reference simulation. This suggests that the overpotential caused by the nSEI is small and grows negligibly under the simulated cyclic load.

This behavior could be explained by the properties of the selected nSEI model, which includes a potential dependence. If the potential difference of the liquid and solid phase is small, the second term of Eq. (5) will have little influence on
j
interst,SEI
, which in turn results in a slower nSEI growth rate in Eq. (6). Nonetheless, with respect to the possible overestimation of the negative electrode potential curve due to aging, the impact of the nSEI model is considered minimal in the simulations performed. In contrast, the pSEI model is identified as a salient factor in the context of model-based charge controllers and aging, given its ability to dominate the transition from CC to CV charging.

Besides the influence of particle crack propagation and SEI growth, the impact of the LAM submodel on the anode potential needs to be investigated. In Fig. 7, the simulation neglecting the LAM submodel, shown in green, indicates that LAM has a significant impact on the negative electrode’s potential in the model, which increases with the C-rate. Referring to Eq. (18), LAM alters the active material volume fraction
ɛ
s
of the respective electrode, which eventually changes the surface-to-volume ratio a . Since the charging current remains constant over the course of the simulated aging, the individual current density increases. This effect, exemplarily annotated in Fig. 7e, decreases overpotentials within the negative electrode when LAM is not considered and influences the charge transfer overpotential as well as the SEI overpotential. In the model examined, this effect is approximately 7 mV at 0.75 C, as indicated in Fig. 7f.

Since neglecting LAM results in a notably elevated potential curve compared to the reference (red, dashed), incorporating the LAM submodel is essential for accurately capturing aging behavior. In the most unfavorable scenario, if LAM is not considered, the surface potential may be overestimated, which could lead to higher charging currents and increased lithium plating risk in virtual sensing applications.

As discussed in Section 4.2, ignoring the LAM submodel in constant C-rate charging leads to inaccurate estimates of the negative electrode surface potential, assuming the reference simulation is accurate. Additionally, the simulations do not impose a controlled, constant surface potential, which is essential for optimizing charging time in model-based fast charging [27,93]. To address this, a simulative Multi-step Constant Current (MCC) charging regime was developed based on the experimentally verified charging behavior of the SLP cell. Starting at 1.5 C, the current is reduced by 1% as the anode surface potential approaches
20
mV
, mimicking Constant Anode Potential (CAP) charging in the virtual cell. The MCC simulation was conducted after 500 simulation cycles in accordance with prior investigations in Section 4.2.1 – 4.2.3. This ensures that cell aging is primarily driven by the experimentally supported moderate C-rate regime (see Section 2.3), with the MCC protocol applied only for final performance evaluation of the aged cell model. Furthermore, it is important to note that the base parameterization of the model without aging has already been examined for its suitability at higher C-rates in previous studies [27].

Fig. 8a depicts the corresponding C-rate over the SoC, while Fig. 8b and Fig. 8c illustrate the predicted half-cell potentials at the interface between the separator and the respective electrode. Fig. 8d displays the simulated full-cell voltage curves.

When particle crack propagation is excluded, the blue model shows minimal deviation from the reference simulation (red, dashed), confirming that crack propagation has little impact on the negative electrode surface potential under CAP charging conditions in the virtual cell. Furthermore, the significant influence of SEI modeling on the full-cell voltage in Fig. 8d and the positive half-cell’s potential curve in Fig. 8c is noteworthy, illustrated again in orange. When the employed SEI growth models are disregarded, a considerable reduction in the model’s overpotentials, particularly at the positive half-cell, becomes apparent. This can be attributed to the proposed pSEI model, which was discussed in Section 3.2.1 and Section 3.2.5, necessary to replicate the potential curves in Fig. 5. However, the seemingly unexpected pattern of minor nSEI impact and dominant pSEI effects has also been experimentally observed in the work of other authors. For instance, Stiaszny et al. [61] studied harvested active material from cylindrical 18650 cells, employing a graphite negative electrode and positive electrode consisting of
LiMn
2
O
4
/ Li
(
Ni
0 . 5
Co
0 . 2
Mn
0 . 3
)
O
2
. The authors concluded that the increase in the impedance of the positive electrode dominates over the impedance change of the negative electrode with increasing age. However, it is noted that disassembly and reconstruction from harvested material may have disturbed the SEI, making this observation potentially process-related. Additionally, electrode stampings were measured after dimethyl carbonate washing between aging and EIS measurement, which may have washed away or damaged the nSEI.

With the present SLP aging study and the in-situ half-cell measurements, these observations regarding the distribution of the resistance increase can indeed be supported, even though the cell of Stiaszny et al. contained a blended material for the positive electrode. Referring to Fig. A.2, which shows the half-cell resolved DC resistance of the applied DC pulses over aging, it appears that the predominant increase in resistance in the SLP cell may originate from the positive half-cell. While the DC-resistance suggests structural changes in the positive electrode, further evidence could potentially be obtained through microscopic methods, which were not included here due to reasons of conciseness and scope. The DC resistance of the negative half-cell remains relatively constant, which is consistent with the minor polarization changes predicted by the aging model.

However, this conclusion may be inconclusive and not yet fully accurate. Due to volumetric work in the material, it is conceivable that the surface area within the active material of the negative electrode could increase, thereby changing the charge transfer resistance [61]. In combination with increasing nSEI thickness, the changes in resistance and the charge transfer kinetics of the cell could establish a balance and thus cancel each other out. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, this theoretically possible effect is not included in any of the DFN-based aging models in the literature and is considered a gap in the modeling efforts of the research community, which also cannot be remedied in this study.

In addition to the considerations regarding SEI and crack propagation, Fig. 8 highlights the influence of LAM in the model, showing significantly higher potential curve, when not considering the LAM submodel (green), compared to the other simulation scenarios in Fig. 8b. This suggests a possible overestimation of the negative electrode potential during charging and may cause an arbitrary model-based charging controller to set a higher current in an attempt to achieve faster charging times. If the ground-truth surface potential is actually lower than simulated, an increased risk of lithium plating arises, which becomes more and more significant if the potential drops below 20 mV. It is important to note that, within the model, this potential difference does not approach 0 mV. This is evidenced by the discrepancy between the red curve of the full model and the green curve of the model neglecting the LAM, which remains below 20 mV. However, this safety margin could decrease in applications with greater thermal and utilization inhomogeneities than the small-scale cells with effective thermal management used in this study. The risk of lithium plating would further increase under more aggressive fast-charging scenarios with higher current densities, particularly when considering locally resolved thermal gradients and utilization heterogeneities [94,95].

This study proposes an aging-sensitive, experimentally validated, physicochemical model for a custom Single-Layer Pouch (SLP) lithium-ion cell with multiple reference electrodes (REs). The utilized
Li
4
Ti
5
O
12
RE eliminates the need for active material harvesting over the course of the aging study, thereby avoiding the risk of altering degradation phenomena. The model accurately replicates the analytically extracted degradation modes, charging capacities, and in-situ measured half-cell potentials over various C-rates.

Importantly, the use of REs revealed a substantial increase in cathode polarization during aging that would likely be overlooked or misinterpreted when modeling degradation based on conventional full-cell measurements. Relying solely on full-cell data may therefore result in an inaccurate assessment of aging mechanisms.

Additionally, advanced physics-based aging submodels for the Doyle-Fuller-Newman model were discussed, and the refinement of their parameters in light of the presented aging study and previous work by various authors was outlined. One-At-A-Time (OAAT) analyses showed that neglecting the submodel describing Loss of Active Material (LAM) leads to overestimating the negative electrode surface potential during charging. It is concluded that prioritizing the modeling of LAM is crucial for the practical implementation of real-time capable models, further enhancing fast charging controllers and their application. Although the measurement results highlight the importance of the pSEI model in replicating the cathode polarization buildup, it is incapable of inducing an overestimation of the cell’s negative electrode surface potential, since it can only precipitate the switchover to CV charging, thereby prolonging charging time.

While the proposed model aligns well with measurements and degradation modes, further work on cathode degradation modeling could be useful. Imaging methods, for instance, might help to refine the models of Zhuo et al. [71] and Ghosh et al. [70] and couple them with other known degradative effects. Furthermore, additional work is needed on modeling mechanical stress and strain to account for more granular, multidimensional effects such as, e.g., anisotropy in material properties, non-uniform mechanical stress within particles, crack coalescence or bifurcation, and the stochastic variability of initial defects. While the experimental and modeling approach presented deliberately focuses on avoiding conditions that lead to lithium plating, future research should investigate how the proposed model can be extended and parameterized to capture plating behavior and its interactions with other degradation mechanisms, particularly LAM. The experimental setup presented here is well suited for this purpose.

In addition to the aforementioned potential model adjustments, it is essential to reconfirm the results of this work with a broader dataset. As outlined in Section 2.5, the current dataset does not permit conclusions regarding the generalizability or statistical representativeness of the proposed aging model’s parameter values, even though it provides meaningful insights into electrode-resolved aging and substantiates the viability of the resource-efficient modeling approach.

Moreover, the difficulty of identifying aging parameters necessitates new methods to improve our understanding of parameter sensitivities, as well as parametric identifiability, and reduce computation time. The authors are currently engaged in research related to this topic.

In summary, the findings presented in this study contribute to a more accurate physicochemical modeling of battery degradation and may guide improvements in operational battery management, particularly for fast charging applications and use cases.
